era_context: 1377 is a year of political transition and consolidation across multiple civilizations. In Western Europe, the aging Edward III of England is approaching the end of his reign, and the question of succession is becoming pressing. The Hundred Years' War continues with reduced intensity. In China, the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power under the Hongwu Emperor. In the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, Ottoman expansion continues. This is a year of relative stability before the religious crisis of 1378.

events.title: Death of Edward III of England: Succession of Richard II
events.description: Edward III of England died on June 21, 1377, after a reign of 50 years (1327–1377). Edward III had been one of the most powerful and influential English kings, initiating the Hundred Years' War and establishing the Order of the Garter. His death marked the end of an era. He was succeeded by his young grandson Richard II (age 10), the son of Edward, the Black Prince, who had died in 1376. Richard II's minority and the regency that followed created political instability and set the stage for the conflicts of the 1380s, including the Peasants' Revolt of 1381. The succession of a child king was a significant political event that would shape English history for decades.
key_figures: Richard II (King of England, age 10 at accession)
John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, regent and uncle of Richard II)
events.certainty_note: The death of Edward III and the succession of Richard II are among the most thoroughly documented events of the 14th century, with contemporary royal records and multiple chronicles providing detailed accounts. The date is certain.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military operations and truces
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued in 1377 with English forces conducting military operations in France. The war had entered a phase of reduced intensity compared to the mid-14th century, with a series of truces, raids, and limited engagements rather than large-scale battles. In 1377, the English were attempting to maintain their territorial holdings in Aquitaine and Calais while conducting raids along the Channel coast. The death of Edward III in June 1377 created some uncertainty in English military strategy, but the war continued under the regency government.
key_figures: Charles V of France (King of France)
events.certainty_note: The broader military context is well-documented; specific engagements in 1377 are attested in chronicles but lack contemporary military records.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his systematic consolidation of Ming power in 1377. The emperor was implementing administrative reforms designed to centralize authority and eliminate the power of regional warlords and the old Yuan bureaucracy. In this year, the Ming court was reorganizing provincial administration and establishing the institutional structures that would define Ming governance for centuries. The Hongwu Emperor was also consolidating military power and establishing the system of imperial examinations that would become central to Ming administration.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (Emperor of China)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records and the official Ming Shilu provide detailed documentation of the Hongwu Emperor's reign and consolidation efforts.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans: Consolidation of territorial gains
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I continued its expansion into the Balkans in 1377. Ottoman forces were consolidating control over territories conquered in previous years and preparing for further expansion. The Ottoman military system, based on the devshirme (levy of Christian boys for military service) and the elite Janissary corps, was becoming increasingly effective. By 1377, the Ottoman Empire was the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, and Christian states in the region were increasingly fragmented and unable to mount effective resistance.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman expansion is well-established, though specific events in 1377 are not well-documented in contemporary sources. Ottoman chronicles are often later compilations with uncertain dating.

events.title: Papal politics: Gregory XI's death and the approach of the Great Schism
events.description: Pope Gregory XI died on March 27, 1378 (not 1377, but this is a correction to note). However, in 1377, the papal court was experiencing significant political tensions. Gregory XI had moved the papal seat back to Rome from Avignon in 1377, a decision that was controversial among the cardinals and contributed to the tensions that would lead to the Great Schism in 1378. In 1377, the question of papal succession was becoming increasingly important, and the cardinals were divided over the future direction of the Church.
events.certainty_note: Gregory XI died in 1378, not 1377. However, 1377 is relevant as a year of political tension related to the succession question and the move of the papal seat to Rome. The dating of specific political events in 1377 is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The succession of Richard II is sometimes presented as a smooth transition, but contemporary sources indicate significant political tension and uncertainty. The regency government was unstable, and various nobles competed for power. The claim that Edward III's death was a major turning point in the Hundred Years' War is somewhat overstated; the war continued with similar intensity under the regency government. Additionally, the move of the papal seat to Rome in 1377 is sometimes presented as a triumphant return, but it was actually controversial and contributed to the tensions that led to the Great Schism.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1377 is rich for Western Europe, particularly regarding the death of Edward III and the succession of Richard II, which are documented in royal records and multiple chronicles. The record is moderately detailed for France (Hundred Years' War) and for China (Ming administrative records). The record is sparse for the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, where Ottoman sources are often later compilations with uncertain dating. The record is minimal for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, the Americas, and Oceania. The year is well-documented for elite political and military history but poorly documented for economic, social, and cultural history outside elite circles. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The succession of the young Richard II in 1377 created political instability that contributed to the Peasants' Revolt in 1381
The succession of Richard II in 1377 set the stage for his eventual deposition in 1399
The papal tensions in 1377 led directly to the Great Schism in 1378
